ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Indian vegetarian thali set; 'curry dish' (FOODON_03315595) used as the closest defensible anchor for the curry component. The full set includes daal, naan, rice, and pakora.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — vegetable curry with legumes and cereal accompaniments
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00KG (composite dish) used as the closest defensible category for a mixed Indian vegetarian meal set comprising curry, daal, naan, rice, and pakora. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Indian vegetarian thali set; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Indian vegetarian set meal comprising a portion of vegetable curry, daal, one naan, a serving of rice, and mixed vegetable pakora, typically weighing approximately 400 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Indian Veg — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Indian Veg' (cr_id G00010661), a canonical Singapore food record with confidence 0.95. Values represent the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Indian Meals — analogous estimate based on rice-based composite Indian meal data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI value exists for this specific Indian vegetarian set meal. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous composite: white basmati rice (GI ~58–65) combined with lentil daal (GI ~29) and naan bread (GI ~71). The weighted composite estimate yields approximately GI 65 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 63.8 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 41.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (15.66 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention, elevating cardiovascular risk despite low sodium (30 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish in its current form due to the high sugar load (15.66 g/100 g with fibre only 0.3 g/100 g); the source of added sugar must be identified and eliminated, and fibre-rich vegetables should replace starchy or sweetened components.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 15.66 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g diabetes hard gate; dietary fibre at only 0.3 g/100 g provides no glycaemic modulation, and carbohydrate at 25.13 g/100 g compounds postprandial glucose risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in its current form; added sugar must be eliminated entirely, starchy vegetables reduced, and fibre-rich vegetables (spinach, broccoli, cauliflower) substituted to lower the glycaemic impact before consumption can be considered.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sugar (15.66 g/100 g) with low fibre (0.3 g/100 g) raises triglyceride and LDL risk; fat content is moderate (3.11 g/100 g) and sodium is low (30 mg/100 g), partially mitigating risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high sugar content driving dyslipidaemia risk; reducing added sugar, increasing dietary fibre, and substituting refined naan with whole-grain alternatives would substantially improve the cardiovascular profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 15.66 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; likely presence of nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, potatoes, chilli) may aggravate RA symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its current form; added sugar should be eliminated, nightshade vegetables (tomato, potato, chilli) monitored for individual symptom response, and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) maximised in the preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indian Vegetarian Thali Set (Indian Veg) is a composite Indian vegetarian meal served in Singapore's hawker centres and Indian restaurants, comprising vegetable curry, lentil daal, naan bread, steamed rice, and mixed vegetable pakora (deep-fried fritters). The dish reflects the South Asian culinary tradition of offering a balanced multi-component meal on a single tray or plate. The vegetable curry is typically prepared by simmering mixed vegetables (potatoes, cauliflower, peas, carrots) in a spiced tomato-onion gravy seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The daal is a slow-cooked lentil preparation, mildly spiced and rich in plant protein. Naan is a leavened flatbread baked in a tandoor or on a griddle, providing a soft, slightly chewy texture. The pakora component adds a crispy, fried element. Nutritionally, the set provides 144 kcal per 100 g with 25.13 g carbohydrate, 3.58 g protein, and 3.11 g fat. The sugar content is notably elevated at 15.66 g/100 g with very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g), which raises significant clinical concern for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten from naan. Calcium (100 mg/100 g) and potassium (136 mg/100 g) are notable micronutrients. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), with a high glycaemic load per standard serving due to the large carbohydrate portion. This dish is popular as a lunch or dinner option in Singapore's Indian food stalls and is culturally significant as an accessible, affordable vegetarian meal.
common_user_queries: Calories in Indian vegetarian set meal
Is Indian Veg suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for 'Indian Veg' (cr_id G00010661) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this composite Indian vegetarian set meal are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010661) as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central was also consulted but does not carry a directly matching composite entry with fatty acid breakdown. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin profile for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central does not carry an exact matching composite entry. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central consulted but no exact match available. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central analogous data for mixed vegetable curry with oil. Sugar content of 15.66 g/100 g is unusually high for a vegetable curry preparation and likely reflects added sugar or sweet sauce in the commercial preparation; this value is preserved from the HPB source without modification. The high sugar content renders this dish clinically unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis in its current form. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no exact GI study exists for this specific composite meal. Cholesterol at 9 mg/100 g is consistent with the presence of ghee or dairy in the preparation despite the vegetarian classification.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, applied multilingual naming, GI estimation, health context, and ontology anchors.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added -1 sentinel documentation to notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document the missing-data basis for every -1 sentinel value (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g), citing HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central as the consulted sources confirming unavailability. This resolves the g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure from iteration 2.
